title: Slower startup time with concurrent podman run
body:
### Issue Description In QEMU project, we use containers to cross compile tests for different architectures. As a result, for every test, we launch a container to run a cross-compiler. This is done as part of build process, and thus multiple `podman run` commands are executed in parallel. While working on it, I noticed that compiling with more or less cores had almost no impact on total time, which was surprising. Looking closer, podman run gets slower as we add more cores, and cpu usage barely increases. Time scale "almost" linearly with number of cores, with cpu being unused, which is usually the result of a lock contention. In practice, a normal podman run takes around 0.2s on my machine. When running concurrently with 4 cores, it takes around 0.5s on average. When running concurrently with 8 cores, it takes around 1s on average. When running concurrently with 16 cores, it takes around 2s on average. Note: my machine has 16 physical cores, so it should stay constant. The same can be observed with rootful and rootless podman. Interestingly, if mixing sudo and non sudo podman run, command is still slowed, but with less impact, suggesting it's definitely something on user side and not kernel related, as different users are not impacted. The same can be observed using --network=host or --privileged flag. The same can be observed with podman 5.4.2 (debian trixie) and 4.9.3 (ubuntu 24.04). Finally, I would like to add that same behavior happens with docker, but it's slower than podman in all cases, and I personally only have interest in podman. To reproduce it: ``` # benchmark tool apt install hyperfine parallel # pull image podman pull docker.io/debian # run with 1 core yes 'hyperfine "podman run --rm docker.io/debian true"' | head -n 1 | parallel -j 100 --bar # run with 4 cores and observe that average time increase yes 'hyperfine "podman run --rm docker.io/debian true"' | head -n 4 | parallel -j 100 --bar ``` This kind of performance analysis is always complex, because it requires to track what is happening during [off cpu](https://www.brendangregg.com/offcpuanalysis.html) time. I managed to create a flamegraph using offcputime-bpfcc. ``` sudo apt install bpfcc-tools git clone https://github.com/brendangregg/flamegraph # spawn many podman run yes 'hyperfine "podman run --rm docker.io/debian true"' | head -n 1000 | parallel -j 8 --bar # meanwhile, capture global offcpu time for 10 seconds sudo bash -c 'ulimit -n 1000000 && offcputime-bpfcc 10 -u -f > stack.folded' ~/flamegraph/flamegraph.pl stack.folded > off.svg ``` Full flamegraph: [download](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29865547/off.svg.gz) && gzip -d && google-chrome off.svg Unfortunately, I have not been able to get call stacks, even after rebuilding podman from sources. This could be due to specificities with `go` routines when performing this kind of work, and I didn't really dive into it. I understand that speeding up startup time for containers is a niche use case, but it would still be interesting to understand what is going on, and how to remove this bottleneck. Does anyone with proper `go` experience could give some hints about how investigate further? Else, any idea where to take a look at on podman side? ### Steps to reproduce the issue Steps to reproduce the issue: ``` # benchmark tool apt install hyperfine parallel # pull image podman pull docker.io/debian # run with 1 core yes 'hyperfine "podman run --rm docker.io/debian true"' | head -n 1 | parallel -j 100 --bar # run with 4 cores and observe that average time increase yes 'hyperfine "podman run --rm docker.io/debian true"' | head -n 4 | parallel -j 100 --bar ``` ### Describe the results you received podman run gets slower and slower as we add more cores. ### Describe the results you expected podman run command time stays constant. ### podman info output
``` host: arch: amd64 buildahVersion: 1.39.3 cgroupControllers: - cpu - memory - pids cgroupManager: systemd cgroupVersion: v2 conmon: package: conmon_2.1.12-4_amd64 path: /usr/bin/conmon version: 'conmon version 2.1.12, commit: unknown' cpuUtilization: idlePercent: 98.32 systemPercent: 0.5 userPercent: 1.18 cpus: 32 databaseBackend: sqlite distribution: codename: trixie distribution: debian version: "13" eventLogger: journald freeLocks: 1667 hostname: pc idMappings: gidmap: - container_id: 0 host_id: 1000 size: 1 - container_id: 1 host_id: 100000 size: 65536 - container_id: 65537 host_id: 200000 size: 1001 uidmap: - container_id: 0 host_id: 1000 size: 1 - container_id: 1 host_id: 100000 size: 65536 - container_id: 65537 host_id: 200000 size: 1001 kernel: 7.0.12+deb13-amd64 linkmode: dynamic logDriver: journald memFree: 19110531072 memTotal: 32921419776 networkBackend: netavark networkBackendInfo: backend: netavark dns: package: aardvark-dns_1.14.0-3_amd64 path: /usr/lib/podman/aardvark-dns version: aardvark-dns 1.14.0 package: netavark_1.14.0-2_amd64 path: /usr/lib/podman/netavark version: netavark 1.14.0 ociRuntime: name: crun package: crun_1.21-1_amd64 path: /usr/bin/crun version: |- crun version 1.21 commit: 10269840aa07fb7e6b7e1acff6198692d8ff5c88 rundir: /run/user/1000/crun spec: 1.0.0 +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +WASM:wasmedge +YAJL os: linux pasta: executable: /usr/bin/pasta package: passt_0.0~git20250503.587980c-2+deb13u1_amd64 version: "" remoteSocket: exists: true path: /run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock rootlessNetworkCmd: pasta security: apparmorEnabled: false capabilities: CAP_CHOWN,CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE,CAP_FOWNER,CAP_FSETID,CAP_KILL,CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE,CAP_SETFCAP,CAP_SETGID,CAP_SETPCAP,CAP_SETUID,CAP_SYS_CHROOT rootless: true seccompEnabled: true seccompProfilePath: /usr/share/containers/seccomp.json selinuxEnabled: false serviceIsRemote: false slirp4netns: executable: /usr/bin/slirp4netns package: slirp4netns_1.2.1-1.1_amd64 version: |- slirp4netns version 1.2.1 commit: 09e31e92fa3d2a1d3ca261adaeb012c8d75a8194 libslirp: 4.8.0 SLIRP_CONFIG_VERSION_MAX: 5 libseccomp: 2.6.0 swapFree: 17670131712 swapTotal: 19752849408 uptime: 41h 27m 1.00s (Approximately 1.71 days) variant: "" plugins: authorization: null log: - k8s-file - none - passthrough - journald network: - bridge - macvlan - ipvlan volume: - local registries: {} store: configFile: /home/user/.config/containers/storage.conf containerStore: number: 164 paused: 0 running: 0 stopped: 164 graphDriverName: overlay graphOptions: overlay.mount_program: Executable: /usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs Package: fuse-overlayfs_1.14-1+b1_amd64 Version: |- fusermount3 version: 3.17.2 fuse-overlayfs: version 1.13-dev FUSE library version 3.17.2 using FUSE kernel interface version 7.40 graphRoot: /home/user/.local/share/containers/storage graphRootAllocated: 1918223511552 graphRootUsed: 785433731072 graphStatus: Backing Filesystem: extfs Native Overlay Diff: "false" Supports d_type: "true" Supports shifting: "true" Supports volatile: "true" Using metacopy: "false" imageCopyTmpDir: /var/tmp imageStore: number: 202 runRoot: /run/user/1000/containers transientStore: false volumePath: /home/user/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes version: APIVersion: 5.4.2 BuildOrigin: Debian Built: 1766335321 BuiltTime: Sun Dec 21 08:42:01 2025 GitCommit: "" GoVersion: go1.24.4 Os: linux OsArch: linux/amd64 Version: 5.4.2 ```
### Podman in a container No ### Privileged Or Rootless None ### Upstream Latest Release No ### Additional environment details _No response_ ### Additional information _No response_

comments.body:
I will take a look, but the first thing that comes to mind is storage locking or a similar issue.

> When running concurrently with 8 cores, it takes around 1s on average.

Does this represent the overall average time for starting 8 containers in parallel, or is it the average start time per container when running up to 8 processes concurrently?

The latter, it's the average time for one single podman run in the concurrency context.
`hyperfine` just runs the command given in parameter several times to give you an average time (+ standard deviation).

There are several central locks, the alive.lck taken for each command for a short time. Then multiple storage lock files that are needed to guard the layer/container creation (consider using https://github.com/podman-container-tools/container-libs/blob/main/storage/contrib/scripts/store-locks.bt to trace them)
there is the central sqlite db where sqlite will have to lock the db for writes. And lastly there is also a central network lock for any network setup/teardown, but that should not be in play for --network=host/--network=none tests.

I assume all of them will contribute to slowing things down but none of them should show a linear grow as there is still a lot of work going on outside the locks.

I guess the more obvious answer would be IO contention, the db state must be synced as well as the storage json db files that all get synced for each update. So the system will end up with lots of small writes and has to sync a lot.
